query: massive blood transfusion complications metabolic alkalosis

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Metabolic Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: Though the physiologic process of shock and massive transfusions suggests the expected development of metabolic alkalosis, some patients can develop metabolic acidosis due to failure of H+/K+ and Na-K-ATP pumps as anaerobic metabolism works to meet ATP demands. These changes force excess potassium from transfused blood products into cells, followed by an efflux of hydrogen ions. These changes, in combination with the excess production of lactate, cause an acidosis. Excess lactate can also bind calcium, worsening hypocalcemia. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Metabolic Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic alkalosis can lead to hypokalemia as hydrogen ions exit cells to offset the alkalosis via an H+/K+ transporter. [29] Alternatively, stored RBCs may contain elevated extracellular potassium, and rapid infusion of older units, especially through central lines, high-volume transfusions, or blood exchange procedures involving heart-lung machines, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) devices, and apheresis machines that use older, cold-stored blood as priming solutions, can precipitate hyperkalemia and arrhythmias; using fresher or washed RBCs mitigates this risk. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Metabolic Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: The use of sodium citrate and citric acid in PRBCs, FFP, and platelets during storage to prevent coagulation can result in metabolic alkalosis and hypocalcemia. [29] [30] [31] Citrate metabolism generates bicarbonate and causes metabolic alkalosis when the kidneys are unable to excrete the excess. Citrate also binds to ionized calcium, potentially causing hypocalcemia, whereas calcium bound to albumin is unaffected. Severe hypocalcemia can lead to paresthesias and cardiac dysrhythmias. Regular monitoring of blood pressure and the corrected QT interval is crucial to prevent complications. Hypocalcemia results in more pronounced coagulopathy, necessitating additional transfusions, extended stays in the ICU, prolonged ventilator use, and heightened mortality rates. The majority of patients do not require calcium supplementation, and the administration of calcium does not demonstrate any benefit in mitigating these effects. However, symptomatic patients warrant treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperkalemia
retrieved_docs.content: A concern in massive transfusions, patients with impaired renal function, and infants, hyperkalemia due to transfusion is likely transient, and the true incidence of complications like arrhythmia and cardiac arrest is unclear. A rapid infusion rate increases the risk, especially through a central venous catheter. Using fresher or washed RBCs and monitoring serum potassium levels can mitigate this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Alkalosis -- Etiology -- Iatrogenic causes:
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperventilation in intubated patients on mechanical ventilation Extracorporeal membranous oxygenation

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Complications due to massive transfusion
retrieved_docs.content: A massive transfusion occurs when a patient receives 10 or more units of RBCs within 24 hours. [28] Common potential complications are dilutional coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, and hypothermia. [29] Clotting factor consumption and activation due to tissue trauma or reduced clotting factor activity due to dilution, prolonged shock, hypoxia-induced acidosis, or hypothermia can all lead to coagulopathy. [29] Packed RBCs do not contain plasma and platelets, allowing them to dilute coagulation proteins and platelets. As a general rule, adults will experience a 10% decrease in the concentration of clotting proteins for each 500 mL of blood loss replaced with plasma-poor RBCs. Additionally, 10 to 12 units of transfused RBCs are associated with a 50% fall in the platelet count. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Complications due to massive transfusion
retrieved_docs.content: As storage time increases, potassium leakage causes extracellular potassium levels in RBCs to rise. Transfusion-related hyperkalemia is uncommon in adults and is more likely to occur in clinical settings involving severe trauma, impaired renal function, and in newborns and infants. Hyperkalemia is common in patients who experience severe trauma due to tissue breakdown and the release of cellular potassium into the extracellular fluid. The additional potassium from blood products, low cardiac output impairing renal function, and hypocalcemia, which can intensify hyperkalemia symptoms, exacerbate this risk. Infants and newborns are particularly vulnerable due to their small blood volume and lower potassium excretion rate. Although severe hyperkalemia due to transfusion is rare in typical clinical settings, using younger and washed RBCs can help mitigate this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Complications -- Electrolyte Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: They can also occur, although these are rare and more likely associated with large volume transfusion. Hypocalcemia can result as citrate, an anticoagulant in blood products, binds with calcium. [31] Hyperkalemia can occur from the release of potassium from cells during storage. Higher risk in neonates and patients with renal insufficiency. [32] Hypokalemia can result as a result of alkalinization of the blood as citrate is converted to bicarbonate by the liver in patients with normal hepatic function.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Management -- Complications -- Metabolic Alkalosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lactated Ringer solution can contribute to metabolic alkalosis due to the conversion of lactate to bicarbonate in the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Autotransfusion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion protocols from donated cross-matched blood remain the standard treatment for the hemorrhagic patient, although it is not without risk and has potential adverse side effects. Transfusion of donated blood carries the risk of disease transmission, citrate toxicity, hyperkalemia, hypothermia, hypomagnesemia, acidosis, sepsis, respiratory failure, and thrombotic complications. [7] [8] [9] [10] For these reasons, autotransfusion has widely been known as an alternative or adjunct to cross-matched transfusion therapy with fewer risks.

retrieved_docs.content: Infection Febrile reactions Acute and delayed hemolytic reactions Anaphylactic reactions Transfusion-associated acute lung injury [13] Transfusion-associated circulatory overload Hypocalcemia or hypoglycemia due to citrate in the preservative Graft-versus-host disease Iron overload

retrieved_docs.content: Arrhythmia and cardiac arrest if blood is not warmed Necrotizing enterocolitis Hyperkalemia Hypocalcemia Acidosis Hypertension Hypotension Hypothermia Hemolysis if the blood is too warm Umbilical vein perforation Bloody stools, abdominal distension, and emesis Thrombosis due to umbilical vein catheter placement [4] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Complications due to massive transfusion
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium citrate and citric acid serve as anticoagulants for packed RBCs, and citrate binds to calcium, potentially lowering serum calcium levels. Newborns and patients who undergo a massive transfusion are at increased risk of hypocalcemia, which can manifest as hypotension, paresthesias, carpopedal spasms, tetanic contractions, and cardiac arrhythmias. To reduce the risk of hypocalcemia during transfusions, clinicians monitor the transfusion rate, blood pressure, and the corrected QT interval. Citrate metabolism occurs in the liver, and individuals with liver disease or ischemia-induced hepatic dysfunction should closely monitor their ionized calcium level. [31] Unless they are symptomatic, most patients do not require calcium replacement. When treatment is necessary, clinicians use calcium chloride or calcium gluconate.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO): Occurs when the volume of the transfused component causes hypervolemia (volume overload).

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Metabolic Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: Citrate itself blunts coagulation dynamics beyond just lowering Ca²+. In vitro work shows that blood exposed to citrate has less and slower thrombin generation and worse platelet aggregation, even after careful recalcification to the same ionized calcium as controls. On TEG, this appears as a prolonged K-time (slower clot kinetics). [29] Citrate can also bind magnesium ions in the blood, reducing ionized magnesium levels. However, the effect is typically milder than with calcium.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Complications due to massive transfusion
retrieved_docs.content: Newborns and infants require special consideration. Mannitol and adenosine, 2 additional preservative agents, may cause osmotic diuresis and nephrotoxicity, respectively, in neonates and young infants. Fresh blood products may also be necessary. Storage of RBCs depletes levels of 2,3-diphosphoglycerate, a molecule in RBCs that helps release oxygen. In infants, the percentage of fetal hemoglobin and the concentration of 2,3-diphosphoglycerate determine the efficiency of oxygen delivery to tissues. The newborn body has not yet developed the ability to replenish 2,3-diphosphoglycerate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base imbalance (اختلال التوازن الحمضي القاعدي) refers to any disturbance in the normal pH regulation of the blood, encompassing both acidosis and alkalosis of metabolic and respiratory origin. Respiratory acidosis (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي) is a specific subtype of this broader category, occurring when hypoventilation leads to carbon dioxide retention, resulting in a decrease in blood pH. Understanding that respiratory acidosis is a form of acid‑base imbalance is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic interventions, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2592
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions are intravenous solutions used for the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, and they are classified within the broader category of blood and related products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and extrarenal blood nitrogen are closely interrelated. Acute kidney injury is clinically associated with both acidosis and hyperkalemia, indicating that patients with sudden loss of renal function frequently develop metabolic acidosis and elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, acidosis and hyperkalemia commonly co‑occur, reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms in renal failure. Acute kidney injury also includes the condition termed extrarenal blood nitrogen, a manifestation of nitrogenous waste accumulation outside the kidneys. Recognizing these linked abnormalities is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients with acute kidney injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: placing respiratory alkalosis as a higher‑order classification within the spectrum of acid‑base disorders. The condition الحماض (acidosis) is also defined as a type of حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي and is known to co‑occur with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم, underscoring the frequent simultaneous presence of acidosis and hyperkalemia in patients with severe fluid‑electrolyte disturbances. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the complex network of metabolic, respiratory, and toxicological factors that influence fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base homeostasis in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as a type of Physical Treatment Methods, placing it within the broader category of physical therapeutic interventions. While blood transfusion can be a valuable clinical procedure, it is also recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of its classification under physical treatment methods and its possible role in postoperative or procedural complications is essential for clinicians to manage risks and optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia), indicating that it falls under the broader category of anemia disorders. In clinical practice, فقر الدم (anemia) is frequently associated with متلازمة فانكوني (Fanconi syndrome), a renal tubular disorder that can lead to impaired reabsorption of nutrients and electrolytes, contributing to the development of anemia. Consequently, patients with Fanconi syndrome may present with various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia when hemorrhagic events occur, highlighting the importance of recognizing the link between renal tubular dysfunction and hematologic abnormalities for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: upper GI hemorrhage. Collectively, these interrelated entities demonstrate that disturbances in fluid volume, electrolyte concentrations, acid‑base balance, and glucose homeostasis are central to the pathophysiology of acute renal failure, pancreatitis, hepatic injury, urinary infections, respiratory disease, and gastrointestinal bleeding, guiding clinicians toward comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of patients with overlapping metabolic and organ‑specific abnormalities.
